title: How much does curation cost?
author: Peter D. Karp
claim: EcoCyc literature biocuration cost $219 per curated article over a 5-year period (May 2011-2016), computed as $2.1M curation cost / 9,606 newly cited publications; that is 6-15% of typical biomedical open-access publication fees ($1,500-$3,500) and 0.088% of an average NIH R01's cost per publication. Author argues curation cost is 'minuscule' relative to the research it indexes.
verification: PMC full text fetched 2026-07-11; exact figures extracted.

title: Crowdsourcing and curation: perspectives from biology and natural language processing
author: Hirschman L, Fort K, Boué S, Kyrpides N, Islamaj Doğan R, Cohen KB
claim: Perspectives/review paper on crowdsourcing in biocuration (micro-tasking, community editing, GWAP), examining expertise needed, participant motivation, feasibility, cost and quality. As summarised in this paper: a crowdsourced drug-indication experiment reached ~$1.75/abstract for five independent judgements each and ~96% precision / ~89% recall; a separate gene-mutation crowd experiment reached ~80% precision / >70% recall for <$1/abstract; the cited EXPERT-curator baseline was ~90% precision / ~70% recall. Manual curation baseline: 15-20 min/abstract at $30/curator-hour ($2.75-$10/paper). Lesson: crowd curation with redundancy can reach expert-competitive quality on some extraction tasks at low unit cost, but recall/precision vary by task and redundancy multiplies unit cost.
verification: REVISION 2026-07-11b: authors CORRECTED (prior draft wrongly attributed this DOI to 'Good BM, Su AI'). OUP full text fetched 2026-07-11; author list, cost/quality figures, and the crowd-vs-expert attribution extracted. The $1.75 drug-indication figure originates in Good et al. 2015 (bav016, separate source) and is here restated as summarised by Hirschman et al.

title: Scaling drug indication curation through crowdsourcing
author: Good BM, Nanis M, Wu C, Su AI
claim: The PRIMARY drug-indication crowdsourcing experiment: ~$1.75 per drug label, 18,775 judgements from 74 Mechanical-Turk workers within 8h over 3000+ HITs, reaching ~96% aggregated accuracy on control HITs (restated by Hirschman et al. 2016 as ~96% precision / ~89% recall). Demonstrates redundant crowd curation reaching expert-competitive quality on an extraction task at ~$1-2/abstract.
verification: Venue/DOI confirmed (OUP Database, bav016); PMC4369375. Cost/throughput figures corroborated across the OUP listing, PMC copy, and Hirschman et al. 2016's summary. Added 2026-07-11b to de-conflate from the Hirschman perspectives paper.

title: UniProt: a worldwide hub of protein knowledge
author: The UniProt Consortium
claim: UniProtKB expert biocuration captures between 35 and 45% of the curatable literature for any given species, rising to 50% for Homo sapiens; expert curation is 'labour intensive', motivating automated annotation to reach comprehensiveness. UniProt prioritises particular high-value, hard-to-predict functional data in its manual effort.
verification: OUP full text fetched 2026-07-11; capture-rate sentence extracted verbatim. NOTE (2026-07-11b): the paper reports a capture RATE and a prioritisation strategy; it does NOT assert an immutable saturation ceiling. Framings like 'saturates', 'fixed corpus', 'hard human ceiling' are the reviewer's reading, not the paper's words -- softened to 'captures well below completeness on the literature examined' where used inline.

title: Empowering biological knowledgebases: advances in human-in-the-loop AI-driven literature curation
claim: Curated biological resources are maintained by only ~100-200 full-time professional biocurators worldwide; curators must find evidence for each gene across >20M PubMed articles and assign from ~40,000 GO terms; e.g. only ~1,400 microRNA articles curated to GO over a decade -- manual curation cannot keep pace with literature growth.
verification: Venue confirmed (OUP Bioinformatics Advances); workforce/scale figures from search snippet, full text not fetched -- treat figures as venue-attributed.

title: CYC: A Large-Scale Investment in Knowledge Infrastructure
author: Douglas B. Lenat
claim: By 1995 roughly a person-century of hand-encoding effort had produced ~10^6 commonsense axioms in Cyc's knowledge base; Cyc is framed as a large-scale, multi-decade manual knowledge-infrastructure investment.
verification: Venue/DOI unambiguous; ACM PDF returned HTTP 403 (not machine-fetchable this session); person-century + ~10^6-axiom figures corroborated across multiple independent secondary quotations of this paper. Full text NOT parsed.

title: Cyc cumulative cost/effort (tertiary aggregation)
claim: Cyc reportedly consumed ~$60M and ~600 person-years by 2002 and >$200M and ~2,000 person-years with ~30M rules/axioms by 2024.
verification: UNVERIFIED at primary venue -- tertiary encyclopedia aggregation, no primary financial disclosure located. Use only as order-of-magnitude, never load-bearing.

title: Expert systems for configuration at Digital: XCON and beyond
author: Virginia E. Barker, Dennis E. O'Connor
claim: XCON/R1 grew to ~6,200 rules over a database of ~20,000 parts, with a large annual rule-churn rate driven by DEC's hardware churn -- a formidable ongoing maintenance task. Crucially, the paper presents XCON as the SUCCESSFUL cornerstone of a growing production family of Digital expert systems, with satisfactory run-time performance; maintainability was the motivation for the RIME re-implementation, NOT a cause of XCON's removal.
verification: Venue/DOI/pages unambiguous; ACM PDF returned HTTP 403 (not parsed this session). REVISION 2026-07-11b: the annual-churn figure is contested between sources -- secondary quotations attribute '~50%/year' variously to this paper and to Soloway et al. AAAI-87, and the GPT-5.6 review reports Barker & O'Connor give ~40%. Figure recorded as 'large, ~40-50%, exact value and attribution unresolved from primary'. The prior draft's claim that XCON was 'killed by maintenance / FUNDAMENTAL' is NOT supported by this paper and has been removed. Full text NOT parsed.

title: Assessing the Maintainability of XCON-in-RIME: Coping with the Problems of a VERY Large Rule-Base
author: Soloway E, Bachant J, Jensen K
claim: XCON's rule base had grown so large that maintainability itself became the research problem; the RIME re-implementation was motivated by the difficulty of changing a very large, interdependent rule base.
verification: Venue confirmed (AAAI-87 proceedings, paper 148); PDF fetched but returned as undecoded binary (not parsed). Thesis attributed from title + AAAI listing.

title: From Freebase to Wikidata: The Great Migration
author: Pellissier Tanon T, Vrandecic D, Schaffert S, Steiner T, Pintscher L
venue: WWW 2016 (25th Int. Conf. on World Wide Web)
claim: Abstract states: 'Due to the success of Wikidata, Google decided in 2014 to offer the content of Freebase to the Wikidata community.' Google's bulk export mapped ~4.6M Freebase topics to Wikidata and generated ~14M candidate statements; migration ran through the open-source Primary Sources Tool where, by Jan 2016, 100+ users had performed ~90,000 approval/rejection actions -- only a small fraction of the candidate statements. Every migrated statement required manual human review.
verification: Venue/DOI confirmed; abstract fetched 2026-07-11 (rationale = 'success of Wikidata'). Figures (4.6M topics, 14M statements, ~90,000 actions, 100+ users, Jan 2016) from the Google-archived copy summary + corroborating sources; full text NOT parsed. REVISION 2026-07-11b: 'provenance verification was THE bottleneck' downgraded to an inference -- the paper reports the per-statement review effort and its slow throughput, not a stated 'bottleneck' verdict or a measured labour cost. The ~90,000 actions cover only a small fraction of the 14M candidates.

title: Google To Close Freebase, Which Helped Feed Its Knowledge Graph
claim: On 16 Dec 2014 Google announced Freebase would be wound down and its data transferred to Wikidata; Freebase went read-only 31 Mar 2015 and closed 2 May 2016. The report frames the move as consolidation around Wikidata/the internal Knowledge Graph.
verification: Contemporaneous trade-press report; shutdown event/dates corroborated by Wikipedia and the WWW-2016 migration paper. REVISION 2026-07-11b: prior draft's phrase 'unsustainability of maintaining a separate open collaborative KB' is NOT a stated rationale in this article -- removed. The migration paper attributes the decision to 'the success of Wikidata'. Secondary source for the event, not a primary cost study and not an economic-unsustainability finding.

title: FreshLLMs: Refreshing Large Language Models with Search Engine Augmentation
author: Vu T, Iyyer M, Wang X, et al.
claim: FreshQA (600 QA pairs requiring fast-changing world knowledge) shows all LLMs struggle on questions needing up-to-date knowledge and produce outdated answers; static parametric knowledge goes stale, and search-augmentation (FreshPrompt) is needed to refresh it. Direct evidence that fact staleness is a first-order, recurring cost.
verification: ACL Anthology listing confirmed (2024.findings-acl.813); arXiv 2310.03214. Claim from abstract/benchmark description.

title: Evaluating the Ripple Effects of Knowledge Editing in Language Models
author: Cohen R, Biran E, Yoran O, Globerson A, Geva M
claim: RippleEdits (5K factual edits) shows prominent parametric knowledge-editing methods fail to introduce consistent changes -- edits do not propagate to logically entailed facts and perturb unrelated ones; a simple in-context (external-context) editing baseline scores best. Parametric fact-update is unreliable; external/context update is more consistent.
verification: ACL Anthology + MIT Press TACL listing confirmed; authors/benchmark size from abstract.

title: Model Editing at Scale leads to Gradual and Catastrophic Forgetting
claim: Sequential ROME/MEMIT edits induce gradual forgetting of prior facts and then an abrupt catastrophic-forgetting phase; neighborhood specificity is lost as edits accumulate. Continuous parametric update of a model's stored knowledge is not a cheap, safe maintenance channel at scale.
verification: arXiv listing confirmed; claim from abstract. Venue verified at arXiv (not the conference PDF).

title: A Study of the Quality of Wikidata
author: Shenoy K, Ilievski F, Garijo D, Schwabe D, Szekely P
claim: Framework to detect low-quality Wikidata statements via three indicators -- community consensus (removed-and-not-re-added statements), deprecated statements, and constraint violations -- surfacing duplicate entities, missing triples, violated type rules; out-of-date information is a recognised, ongoing quality problem in a live collaboratively-maintained KB.
verification: Venue/authors confirmed (JWS special issue; arXiv 2107.00156); abstract fetched. Exact staleness percentages NOT extracted (results not in the fetched abstract) -- any numeric staleness figure is UNVERIFIED.

title: Knowledge Graph Refinement: A Survey of Approaches and Evaluation Methods
author: Heiko Paulheim
claim: Large-scale KGs (DBpedia, YAGO, NELL, Freebase, Google KG) make an explicit trade-off between completeness and correctness; automatic construction leaves errors and gaps that require a continuous refinement/correction stage (error detection + completion) evaluated by precision/recall/AUC. Maintenance is a standing, never-finished cost, not a one-time build.
verification: Venue/DOI/pages confirmed via IOS Press Semantic Web + dblp; thesis from abstract/survey scope.

title: Cheap and Fast -- But is it Good? Evaluating Non-Expert Annotations for Natural Language Tasks
author: Snow R, O'Connor B, Jurafsky D, Ng AY
claim: Across five NLP tasks, aggregating a small number (as few as 4) of paid non-expert Mechanical Turk annotations per item reaches expert-level agreement at a fraction of expert cost, with bias-correction further improving quality. Foundational human-review cost model: quality is buyable with redundancy, but redundancy multiplies per-item cost.
verification: ACL Anthology D08-1027 confirmed; authors/finding from abstract.

title: The Art of Artificial Intelligence (knowledge-acquisition bottleneck)
author: Edward A. Feigenbaum
claim: The binding constraint on building knowledge-based systems is the 'knowledge-acquisition bottleneck' -- the cost and difficulty of eliciting and hand-encoding expert knowledge -- a diagnosis coined by Feigenbaum that framed why hand-curated systems did not scale.
verification: Attribution widely established but the primary IJCAI-77 text was NOT fetched this session; treat the coinage as attributed, the '60% of projects fail / 70-80% of effort on KA' folklore figures as UNVERIFIED.
